title: Social Learning in Prosumption: Evidence from a Randomized Field Experiment
abstract: Digital technologies enable consumers to actively participate in the design and production process for a wide range of products, giving rise to the concept of a ‘prosumer.’ A significant portion of the value for such products is generated through the prosumption process. A variety of firms are investing in building such capabilities; however, a major, largely unexplored, friction in prosumption is the customers’ effort involved to undertake a creative exercise of designing products and extracting value from the process. In this study, we ask whether and how social learning—the act of showing creations made by other customers to focal customers—can ameliorate such friction. Arguably, by showing others’ product designs to focal customers, especially if such designs are attractive and of high quality, the firm may entice users to initiate the design process. Showing others’ designs is also likely to influence customers’ beliefs about their own ability—their self-efficacy—to design a valuable product that they would like to purchase. Certain designs may be perceived as out of the creative reach of focal users and therefore reduce their likelihood of finishing the design and purchasing it. How does this understanding vary across different stages of the purchase funnel? In close collaboration with an e-commerce platform that specializes in customized photo products, we examine the effectiveness of social learning by means of two large scale in-vivo randomized field experiments. We exogenously vary both the availability of others’ designs and the characteristics of the images shown to the treated users. Our analysis shows that showing other users’ designs as examples can be highly effective in influencing the purchase and design behavior of focal customers, but firms must choose therightcustomers and carefully select thetypeof user image design for display. In the upper stages of the purchase funnel, showing high-quality designs to these customers significantly increases the likelihood of project creation. Lower down in the funnel the self-efficacy effect dominates, and users are more likely to finish designs and purchase products when shown designs that are easy to learn.

author.Jaehwuen Jung: Fox School of Business, Temple University, Philadelphia, Pennsylvania 19122;
author.Tianshu Sun: Cheung Kong Graduate School of Business, Beijing 100006, China;
author.Ravi Bapna: Carlson School of Management, University of Minnesota, Minneapolis, Minnesota 55455;

article.1. Motivation: Our partner site for this study is one of a growing number of firms based on a prosumption model that blends the traditional notion of consumers and producers. A prosumer is defined as a consumer who is integrally involved in the design and production process of a product (Moreau 2011,Chandler and Chen 2015,Karmarkar and Roels 2015,Lang et al. 2021).Ritzer and Jurgenson (2010)claim that prosumption has become a salient characteristic of ecommerce and is increasingly adopted by leading brands and retailers such as Nike, Zazzle, and Cafepress.2With advances in Internet do-it-yourself services and digital toolkits, customers can increasingly participate in the production and creation of wide range of products and services.Yadav and Pavlou (2014)andRetana et al. (2015)illustrate this for self-service technologies and software such as Photoshop, whereasArora et al. (2008)do so for experience goods. We investigate the creative context of self-designed products such as photo-based designs on blankets, mugs, bags, home décor, and so on, through our field site. For such products, a significant portion of the value is created through the customer engaging in the creative process of designing a unique product, which is then manufactured and shipped by the platform.3 The prosumption model is not without risk, as it is critically contingent on consumers exerting a significant cognitive effort and possessing a certain creative skillset to design a product that they will find appealing enough to purchase. In creative settings like ours, this can be costly and demanding. Customers may simply choose not to engage with this model if they believe they cannot generate value through the prosumption process (Xue and Field 2008,Retana et al. 2015,Sun et al. 2018). As creative product design process requires both skills in the form of design creativity and knowledge of aesthetic features and technology use, customers may have uncertainty about their own ability to create a quality design and how the final product will appear. The spectrum of uncertainty is likely to affect both their design initiation and purchase decisions. This paper aims to lower barriers to prosumption by using social learning as a means of kickstarting the creative process of product design and consumption. We operationalize social learning by showing focal users specific product designs created by other users on the website. Our social learning interventions, delivered through randomized control trials (detailed later inSection 4), are related to, yet substantially different from, the use of social proof (Zhang 2010,Chen et al. 2011,Tucker and Zhang 2011,Lee et al. 2015,Godinho de Matos et al. 2016,Burtch et al. 2017,Chen et al. 2018,Li and Wu 2018,Sun et al. 2021) to motivate users’ actions. Classical social-proof manipulations often leverage the idea that people want to behave similar to those around them.Burtch et al. (2017)operationalize this in the context of generating user reviews by telling focal users “3,786 other users have written a review.” The signal here is that a multitude of other users are behaving in a certain way so they should too (Muchnik et al. 2013,Lee et al. 2015). In contrast, in a creative setting such as ours and in most other prosumption processes, it is critical to show focal users not only that other users are creating designs but also the types of designs they are actually creating. Thus, our intervention displays concrete examples of creative product designs made by other users (see Online Appendices A and B for the actual manipulation). These user-created designs can be characterized within a space of image characteristics (Shin et al. 2020,Zhou et al. 2021). Such social learning features richer information than a simple or aggregate signal in the use of social proof, that is, the number of others’ purchases or the quantity and/or quality of reviews. Overall, our treatment of social learning captures the nuances of motivating users to engage in creative exercises and to see enough utility in them make a purchase. One significant distinction in the prosumption process is that even if a focal user decides to create a product, they may opt not to purchase the final product. To study this more complex decision funnel, we pay attention not just to the final outcome measure (a review submission or a product purchase, as is often the case in social proof literature) but examine both the initial decision to engage in prosumption (i.e., project creation) and the final outcome (i.e., purchase decision). Specifically, we posit two mechanisms for how showing others’ designs affects different stages of the purchase funnel. First, by seeing other users’ designs, focal users can learn aboutquality,which likely shapes the perceived quality of their final product and influences their initial decision to engage in the design process. This is especially significant for customers without prior design experience, as they have high uncertainty about how the final product will look. We call this theperceived qualityeffect. Second, various technical-, esthetic-, and layout-related features in designs will also affect users’ self-efficacy, that is, their belief in their own ability to create a satisfying product design. This is likely influenced by the perceiveddifficultyof others’ designs and will govern their project completion and eventual purchase likelihood after resolving some uncertainty about quality during the design process. We term this theself-efficacyeffect. We expect different image aspects, such as image quality and difficulty levels, to have differential effects at the upper and lower stages of the purchase funnel. Overall, we achieve our research objective of shedding light on whether and how firms can leverage social learning to influence prosumption. Specifically, we address the following four questions: (Main Effect) What is the effect of showing other customers’ designs on focal customers’ project creation and purchase behavior? (Moderating Effects) How does the effect of showing other customers’ designs change based on focal customers’ prior experience with prosumption? (Design Characteristics) How do image characteristics such as image quality and design difficulty relate to focal users’ prosumption behaviors? (Funnel Differential) Is there a differential effect of image quality and design difficulty on focal users’ prosumption behaviors in upper versus lower stages of the purchase funnel? Given the nuanced theoretical predictions for the effect of social learning, we resolve the research question using two carefully designed, large-scale in vivo randomized experiments (Aral and Walker 2011,Bapna and Umyarov 2015) in collaboration with an online platform that specializes in customized photo products. In Experiment 1, we randomly vary whether to show other users’ designs to all visitors to the website at an individual level. Beyond this baseline randomization, we construct a gallery featuring other users’ actual designs and randomly vary the image features, focusing on the image quality and difficulty dimensions that are shown to users. Such additional randomization allows us to create exogenous variations in quality and difficulty levels on top of the baseline randomization and helps test our hypothesis. In addition, we examine how treatment manifests as a function of customers’ prior experience with the platform. In Experiment 2, we aim to pinpoint the differential effects of the mechanisms at the two stages of the purchase funnel. We focus on two groups of users with different project experience: those who quit before initiating a project (at the browsing stage) and those who quit during the design phase after starting a project (at the project creation stage). We deploy an email-based social learning intervention to these users varying image quality and difficulty levels using a full-factorial design while keeping a control group that was sent an email without any sample designs. The results of our analysis, based on two large-scale field experiments, are in line with our theoretical predictions. First, our results show that at an aggregate level, showing other users’ designs can be highly effective in positively influencing the purchase and design behavior of focal customers. Second, we find that social learning is mostly effective for users with previous design experience. Finally, we find that image quality is a significant driver of outcomes for new customers in the upper stage of the purchase funnel (i.e., perceived quality effect), and difficulty level is more important at the lower or actual purchase stage of the funnel (i.e., self-efficacy effect). Overall, the results show that firms must show the right type or images to therightcustomers at the appropriate stages of the purchase funnel. Our study makes several theoretical and practical contributions. First, our study enriches the literature on customer cocreation (Kumar and Telang 2011,Moreau 2011,Karmarkar and Roels 2015) by investigating whether social learning can improve customers’ prosumption behaviors. In addition, we disentangle the differential effect of two possible image characteristics that map to the consumers utility-maximizing behavior across two critical stages of the purchase funnel. This increases our understanding of the general term “social learning” and gives us practical guidance on how to deploy it. Second, our study builds on prior work that has used digital tools and education campaigns to enable and encourage customers’ prosumption, thereby reducing production costs, improving satisfaction, and ultimately increasing sales (Xue et al. 2011). Previous studies have largely focused on standard coproduction with fixed procedures, for example, Internet banking (Xue et al. 2011), health insurance (Kumar and Telang 2011), and customized preferences (Huang et al. 2017). Extant work has examined the effect of proactive education (Retana et al. 2015), work allocation (Roels 2014), contract design (Roels et al. 2010), and design constraints (Moreau and Dahl 2005) in addressing the previous challenges; however, to the best our knowledge, no research has addressed how firms may leverage multidimensional social learning to help customers overcome challenges in a creative prosumption process such as customized design. Our empirical strategy and experiment design is closely related to our framework and detailed discussions, fully modeling prosumption in a creative context. By doing so, we provide guidance to firms in identifying the appropriate conditions to leverage social learning in prosumption.
article.2. Literature Review: Our study is closely related to two streams of research: value and challenges of customer cocreation and firms’ support of customer cocreation. 2.1. Value and Challenges of Customer Cocreation With rapid developments in online toolkits and cocreation infrastructure, consumers can increasingly adopt the role of prosumer and participate in the creation process of products. A small and emerging stream of literature shows that consumers are voluntarily becoming the designers of their own products across a wide range of categories and shape various aspects of the product designs themselves (Moreau 2011,Karmarkar and Roels 2015). Brands and retailers are actively embracing prosumption. The hope is that products from the customer cocreation process can embody customers’ individual and idiosyncratic tastes and thereby provide more value. The terms cocreation, self-design, and user-design have emerged in the information systems, strategy, operations management, and marketing literature to describe this shift in value in the cocreation process from the firm to the consumer (Moreau and Herd 2010,Kumar and Telang 2011,Xue et al. 2011). Previous empirical research suggests that consumers often show a stronger preference for their own individual designs (Franke et al. 2009) than to fixed off the rack options offered by firms. This can arguably be attributed to the superior fit with individual consumer preferences that self-designed products provide (Kumar and Telang 2011). This stream of research argues that consumers understand their own preferences in a way that professional designers at firms do not. Furthermore, there is also the sense of achievement/accomplishment consumers feel when they successfully complete the design process (“I made it myself”;Franke et al. 2010,Troye and Supphellen 2012). Finally, the design ability and innovation may also better represent consumers’ identities (Moreau and Herd 2010,Atakan et al. 2014) and increase their self-image. In summary, these studies point to the fact that presumption, enabled and amplified by digital technologies in computer-mediated environment (Yadav and Pavlou 2014), increases consumers’ satisfaction with self-designed products and ultimately boosts a firms’ revenue and profits. As mentioned in the Motivation section, despite all the potential benefits, there are significant challenges involved with the cocreation process in the prosumption model. For instance,Heidenreich et al. (2015)explored the dark side of customer cocreation and depicted the negative consequences of failed cocreated services. The challenges they articulated fall into four categories. First, prior research has shown that customers differ in their ability to participate in even the simplest self-service process such as Internet banking (“customer efficiency”;Xue et al. 2011). Such differences in ability lead them to perceive the cost of self-service differently and require different levels of effort when engaging in cocreation. Second, in addition to the efforts needed to participate in self-service, a cocreation task also involves nontrivial innovation in the design process (Burroughs et al. 2008). Additionally, there is often a mismatch between consumers’ own innovation and the requirement of a creative task (Moreau and Herd 2010). Unless customers can learn new ideas and gain inspiration from external sources, such mismatches will likely have a significant negative influence on the process and outcome. Finally, customers may host a subjective evaluation of their own abilities and knowledge for cocreation. Such subjective evaluation may be above or below the customers’ actual level and thus could discourage or encourage the cocreation process. These challenges call for support from firms throughout the cocreation process to encourage product designs and stimulate purchases from consumers with different levels of ability, product expectation, and self-efficacy. We explore these challenges in the hitherto unexplored area of creative goods. 2.2. Previous Research on Firms’ Support in the Cocreation Process Previous research has suggested several approaches to mitigate challenges in customer cocreation. One stream of studies has shown that some level of guidelines and even constraints offered by firms can aid customer cocreation (Moreau and Dahl 2005). For instance,Randall et al. (2007)found that the format of the cocreation task (feature-based or need-based) might significantly influence the cocreation process and outcome (Roels 2014).Hildebrand et al. (2014)introduced the customization via starting solutions (CvSS) architecture, which could help customers substantially reduce the complexity of product cocreation while preserving all its advantages.Dahl and Moreau (2007)demonstrated the importance of constraints (e.g., instructions, target outcomes) in facilitating a balance between perceived competence and autonomy for consumers involved in a creative task (Dellaert and Stremersch 2005). When consumers engage in creative activities with a sense of both autonomy and competence, they enjoy the experience more.Moreau and Dahl (2005)examined how time constraints influence the way in which consumers process information during a creative task and how those processes, in turn, influence the creativity of the solution. Paradoxically, they find that input constraints encourage more creative processing, provided the individual is not under significant time constraints. In addition to guidelines and constraints, previous literature has also investigated the role of incentive and contract design in facilitating and encouraging customer cocreation.Burroughs et al. (2011)found that extrinsic rewards offered in isolation undermine the creative process by reducing intrinsic motivation; however, two experiments in the study further demonstrate that the effect of rewards can be made positive if offered in conjunction with appropriate training. BothXue and Field (2008)andRoels et al. (2010)identified the importance of contract design in encouraging service coproduction. To date, very little attention has focused on how firms can leverage wisdom in the crowd and design a social intervention—for example, showing other users’ designs—to encourage and facilitate the project cocreation behavior. Our study addresses this gap in the literature.
article.3. Mechanisms and Hypothesis: To theoretically situate social learning in prosumption, we draw on previous literature and posit that showing others’ designs as examples could affect customers’ product design process and purchase decisions through two mechanisms. We provide a conceptual framework that describes these two mechanisms inFigure 1. 3.1. Perceived Quality Effect A large stream of literature in economics and psychology supports that users are more inclined to engage in a task if they believe that their efforts will lead to high-quality results (Prendergast 1999,Ryan and Deci 2000,Hendricks et al. 2012).Wigfield and Eccles (2000)suggest that an individual’s willingness to engage in a task is influenced by the perceived likelihood of success and the value attributed to that success. Several studies suggested that high-quality outputs could serve as a signal that sets the bar for acceptable quality, therefore influencing consumers’ decisions to engage in a task (Sweeney et al. 1999,Kirmani and Rao 2000). In our context, showing other users’ designs as examples may allow focal customers to quickly form an expectation around the overall quality of the final product. Specifically, focal consumers observing high-quality designs created by others may establish an elevated set of expectations that encourages engagement in the design process. Furthermore, this would be particularly salient among consumers without prior experience due to the high degree of uncertainty they face. First-time consumers are more influenced by the quality of others’ designs, as they rely on external cues to estimate the final quality of their own prospective projects (Alba and Hutchinson 1987). High-quality designs by others therefore serve as a benchmark, helping inexperienced consumers in overcoming initial hesitation and uncertainties. However, exposure to low-quality designs may discourage them from engaging in the design process. This maps to the top left quadrant ofFigure 1. Conversely, for consumers who have created a project before (or who abandoned while creating a project in the past), they would be less likely to change their expectation based on other’s design and therefore would not highly be affected by the quality of others’ designs. Instead, their decision to reinitiate the design will be impacted by how difficult others’ designs are to create. This would map to how much effort they have to put in and whether they can get net positive utility from the overall process. This corresponds to the bottom left quadrant ofFigure 1. We refer to this effect as the perceived quality effect. 3.2. Self-Efficacy Effect The role shift from customer to prosumer in the cocreation process may prompt focal customers to make comparisons with other customers’ designs on the platform in terms of project output and skills. Research has shown that customers’ innate drive to look externally for comparison is strong (Mussweiler 2003,Mussweiler et al. 2004), and the outcome of these comparisons influences evaluations of their own self-designed products. For instance,Mochon et al. (2012)proposed that creating custom products fulfills consumers’ psychological needs to signal competence to themselves and to others and that feelings of competence associated with self-created products lead to their increased valuation. In our context, once users initiate the design process, they can resolve some uncertainty about quality and gain a better understanding of whether they can create enough value in the creation process to justify a purchase. Design elements such as the theme, layout, and feature use (e.g., text box, background) in others’ creations may also serve as a benchmark and allow focal users to gauge the difficulty of the prosumption process in relation to their own abilities. When customers encounter designs from other users with stronger skills and better innovations that are hard to imitate (upward comparison), they may learn from the design but at the same time may incur a negative feeling from a self-efficacy perspective. While research examining the influence of upward social comparisons on individuals’ behavior has produced unequivocal results (Johnson and Stapel 2007,Pinkus et al. 2008), much of the research on upward comparisons has focused on how individuals cope with the threats they create, indicating that upward comparisons generate negative information about the self (Johnson and Stapel 2007,Moreau and Herd 2010). Therefore, the lower the difficulty level of others’ designs, the higher the user conversions will be at the later stages of the purchase funnel. This corresponds to the right side ofFigure 1. We call this the self-efficacy effect. 3.3. Hypothesis Development Showing others’ designs as examples would activate the two mechanisms; therefore, the overall effect of the treatment may vary. Additionally, a differential effect is possible at the upper stage of the purchase funnel, where users must decide tocreatea project, as opposed to the lower stage, where the decision topurchasethe designed product is made. Consequently, we formulate hypotheses to provide a theoretical understanding of these effects. In terms of the main effect, if one is strictly following the social-proof literature (Zhang 2010;Chen et al. 2011,2018;Tucker and Zhang 2011;Lee et al. 2015;Godinho de Matos et al. 2016;Burtch et al. 2017;Li and Wu 2018;Sun et al. 2021), setting aside the potential bidirectional effect on perceive quality effect as a starting point, social learning is likely to positively influence customers’ purchase and project creation decisions. Thus, we can derive the following hypothesis. (Main Effect).Showing others’ product designs as examples has a positive effect on customers’ behaviors in the prosumption process. Although we expect the overall effect of social learning to be positive, the size of the effect may vary based on customers’ perceived quality formed by their prior experience designing products on the platform and their self-efficacy of the product design. Therefore, it is crucial for the prosumption platform to identify the effectiveness of social learning for different customers, understand the underlying mechanisms and leverage them accordingly. Specifically, it is reasonable to expect that customers who have prior experience with prosumption already have a higher understanding of the quality of the final product thus may additionally benefit from social learning. These users may not attenuate their expectations even if they are exposed to low-quality designs and gain knowledge and increase self-efficacy. Conversely, customers with no prior prosumption experience may be negatively affected when exposed to low-quality designs and negatively adjust their perceived quality. Thus, we derive the following hypothesis. (Moderating Role of Customer Experience).Showing others’ product designs has a positive effect on the prosumption process for customers with prior prosumption (i.e., project creation) experience as compared with customers without prior experience. This hypothesis suggests that firms may selectively leverage social learning in the prosumption process and target those customers with prior experience. However, how should firms choose which designs to show focal customers? As mentioned previously, in the upper stage of the purchase funnel where they decide to engage in project creation, new customers are likely to be heavily influenced by the quality level of others designs. For these customers, the quality of others’ designs acts as an important reference point, influencing their willingness to engage in their own projects. Although high-quality designs from others encourage them to proceed, low-quality designs deter them. Conversely, experienced customers are less swayed by the quality of others’ designs; their focus is more on the difficulty level of the designs when deciding whether to start a new project. Thus, we derive the following hypothesis. (Perceived Quality Effect in Social Learning).For customers without prior project creation experience, showing others’ product designs with high- (low)-quality image features has a positive (negative) effect on customers initiating the prosumption process. (Perceived Quality Effect in Social Learning).For customers with prior project creation experience, showing others’ product designs with low difficulty image features has a positive effect on initiating the prosumption process. In terms of difficulty, the more low-difficulty features—which can be easily emulated by focal customers in their own project creation—present in a design, the more they may facilitate learning and increase prosumption behavior. In contrast, the exposure of certain designs with high-difficulty features may negatively affect self-efficacy and dampen prosumption behavior.4Customers may wonder whether they can make a design that looks as good as the ones shown to them. In terms of the purchase funnel, once they start creating the project, the act of project creation is likely to give them a better sense of their expected costs, and if the others’ images are difficult to imitate, they are unlikely to reach and complete the lower stage of the purchase funnel and purchase the product. Therefore, the difficulty level plays an important role on the later stage of the purchase funnel. This leads us to hypothesize the following. (Self-Efficacy Effect in Social Learning).Showing others’ product designs with low-difficulty image features has a positive effect on customers completing the prosumption process.
article.4. Institutional Details and Experimenta: 4.1. Institutional Details We collaborate with a large U.S.-based online platform that specializes in customized photo products. The platform has a large number of visitors (tens of millions per year). Customers can create and purchase a variety of customized photo products on the platform (e.g., photo canvases, blankets, etc.). A defining aspect of the context is that all product offerings on the platforms require customers’ creative design efforts during the production process. Any purchased product consists of two parts: a customized design created by the customer (i.e., prosumption part) and a physical material to realize this design (i.e., firm production part). In other words, the value of any product sold by the platform is partially determined by the customer’s own creation. Thus, customers might not purchase a product if they think they cannot create enough value in the prosumption part. Customers can innovate in a few ways during the cocreation process, including (a) choosing photos for the project, (b) arranging and adjusting the layout of photos in the design (e.g., size and location of each photo), and (c) adding additional features (e.g., background, shapes, and colors). Prior to this study, the platform did not have any other users’ designs displayed publicly as examples. 4.2. Experiment 1 In Experiment 1, we randomly assigned all website visitors who browse the product page into control group and treatment group, with equal probability. Only customers in the treatment group were able to see a gallery containing other users’ designs on the site. For each subject in the treatment, we randomly chose 9 designs from the pool of 74 solicited designs that represented customer innovations5and presented them in random order. To better understand which images with different quality and difficulty levels best affect customer cocreation behavior, we only showed the top three images to customers and allowed them to then further expand the gallery to see more images (Online Appendix, Figure A.1).6In this way, we not only vary the availability of the treatment (binary indicator), but also create exogenous variation in the product features of the treatment (high-dimensional policy space). Our randomization is at the user level and all users in the treatment see the identical set of designs each time they visit the website.7We conducted the experiment for three months, and the experiment involved more than 88,000 visitors to the platform. 4.3. Experiment 2 In Experiment 2, we selected unique customers who visited the platform but left without making a purchase and randomly assigned them into one of the five test groups (Table 1). To pinpoint the effect of showing sample designs as consumers move through the purchase funnel, we selected customers who abandoned their project during two phases of the purchase funnel: the browsing stage and the project creation stage.Browsing stagedenotes customers who dropped out while browsing the website (i.e., with no project experience) andproject creation stagedenotes customers who left the website after starting a project (i.e., with project experience). Then, we intervened by sending emails to these subjects with exogenous variations in image quality and difficulty. To design the treatment, we closely worked with the design team at the collaborating platform and created four versions of designs that varied in terms of difficulty (high versus low) and quality (high versus low).8Then, we created five versions of emails9by only changing the designs shown in the email, as shown in the Online Appendix, Figure A.2. We included a 50% discount coupon in all emails to encourage customers to return to the website, which was standard practice on the platform at the time of the study. The email was sent two hours after focal customers left the website, and we tracked whether those customers further created and purchased a product within three days after the email was sent. Table 1.Five Test Groups in Experiment 2 Table 1.Five Test Groups in Experiment 2 The value of conducting Experiment 2 is twofold. First, the design of Experiment 2 allows us to examine how social learning effects vary among customers with different experiences, based on the stages of the purchase funnel where they discontinued the process. Second, by creating four treatments that vary based on quality/difficulty levels, we can directly compare the effect of showing different designs to that of not showing any designs (control group) that was not possible in Experiment 1. We conducted the experiment for seven months. Overall, we sent emails to 201K customers on the platform and conducted the analysis based on 34K customers who opened the email.10
article.5. Empirical Strategy: 5.1. Key Constructs We constructed a series of customer characteristics and image characteristics to test the hypothesized mechanisms in our empirical analysis. First, we match the data from our field experiment with the rich archival data from the platform and construct customer characteristics using their historical behaviors and interactions with the platform. Specifically, we characterize the prior experience of customers with the product and with the cocreation process based on their past project creations. Second, to examine how different levels of quality and difficulty in images influence the effect of social learning, we followed a careful procedure and conducted two surveys on Amazon Mechanical Turk (MTurk). For Experiment 1, we conducted a survey to understand users’ perceptions regarding quality and difficulty dimensions of sample designs used in the gallery. In this way, we obtained a granular measure of the nature of the treatment. Instead of purely relying on the binary variableTreatmentto indicate whether a user is exposed to others’ designs or not, we identify the level of quality and difficulty of the designs shown to each specific user to understand the effect of image features on customer behavior. For Experiment 2, we conducted another survey to ensure that customers perceived variations in terms of quality and difficulty in our four treatment designs. We have detailed the coding process and provided examples used in the experiments in Online Appendix B. 5.2. Outcomes of Interest We focus on three key outcomes: (a) customers’ project design efforts (e.g., the number of projects created or revised),11(b) customers’ purchase behaviors (e.g., the number of orders), and (c) the revenue generated by the firm.12All three outcome variables may be affected by others’ designs and are closely interrelated. The first two outcomes characterize customer behaviors, whereas the last outcome characterizes the firm’s outcome. We collect data on the three outcomes and further augment the experiment data with rich archival data, including past purchases and project creation behavior for each customer. 5.3. Empirical Strategy Our randomization ensures that focal customers’ exposure to other’s designs is orthogonal to their previous behaviors. Therefore, any difference in customers’ purchase and project creation decisions can be solely and directly attributed to the difference in the exposure to others’ designs. To estimate the treatment effect, we perform regression analysis at the customer level. Specifically, to examine the net effect of showing others’ designs on focal customers’ decisions, we relate the outcome variables to the dummy indicator of our treatment groups and use count model for the number of projects and orders, as well as ordinary least squares for revenue (Equation (1)). Our main estimation equation for customeriis Additionally, to examine how customer characteristics moderate the effect of exposure to others’ designs, we interact the moderating variable with the treatment group indicator and estimate the model using the following specification (Equation (2)): Finally, we then further examine the role of quality and difficulty levels in others’ designs in influencing social learning process and estimate the model using the following specification (Equation (3)) using treatment group data set The vector𝐶𝑜𝑙𝑙𝑎𝑔𝑒𝐶ℎ𝑎𝑟𝑖,𝑐CollageChari,crepresents a vector of measures on the level of exposure to design features for useri.13The coefficient𝛽𝑣,𝑐βv,ccaptures the effect of certain design featuresc(as discussed inSection 4.1) on customer behaviors. This approach allows us to better understand what type of example designs are more effective on customer cocreation and future purchase behavior.
article.6. Analysis and Results: 6.1. Experiment 1 We first check the validity of randomization usingttests across a range of customer characteristics. The results of the comparison tests are reported inTable 2, which shows no statistically significant difference between experimental groups in any aspect. The randomization check confirms that the pre-experiment purchase level for customers is comparable across the control and treatment groups, indicating that our randomization was successful. Table 2.Randomization Check (Experiment 1) Table 2.Randomization Check (Experiment 1) 6.1.1. Main Effect of Social Learning. We examine the causal effect of showing others’ designs on customers’ cocreation and purchase behaviors by directly comparing the key outcomes across the control and treatment groups. The results are presented inTable 3and show that, at an aggregate level, exposure to others’ designs leads to a significant increase in their project creation and purchase decisions (Table 3, columns 1–3). This validatesHypothesis 1. Table 3.Causal Effect of Showing Other Customers’ Designs (Experiment 1) Table 3.Causal Effect of Showing Other Customers’ Designs (Experiment 1) Note.Robust standard errors are in parentheses. *, **, and ***Significant at 10%, 5%, and 1%, respectively. To validate that the treatment is indeed the cause for the changes quantified inTable 3, we conducted additional analysis to examine whether users in the treatment group made similar design choices to the treatment design (e.g., the other users’ designs they were shown) when creating their own design. Specifically, we worked with our partner company to collect additional data regarding several technical and design features used in photo projects and constructed a vector for each design used for the treatment, in addition to those created by the users in the experiment. Then we computed the similarity between two sets of designs—that is, if the design the focal user created, and the treatment design assigned to her are similar—using the cosine similarity measure. After that, we checked whether the similarity measure is different from the similarity measure created based on the focal user’s treatment design and the designs created by randomly selected users in the control group (who were not exposed to any treatment designs) and those created by other users in the treatment group (who were exposed to different treatment designs). As presented in Table C.3 in the Online Appendix, we find that designs created by focal users were more similar to their treatment designs than designs created by other users. Our results provide empirical evidence that users in the treatment group imitated design choices in the treatment when creating their own designs. 6.1.2. Role of Customers’ Prior Experience. FollowingHypothesis 2, we perform moderating effect analysis based on customers’ prior experience with prosumption as revealed by their past project creation behaviors. First, we interact the customer’s past project creation indicator with treatment group indicator (Equation (2)). The results are reported inTable 4. We find that past purchase behavior positively moderates the treatment effect on customers’ project creations and purchases (Table 4, columns 1–3). Those who have created a product in the past benefit more from exposure to others’ designs. In contrast, for those who never purchased before but just accessed the website, exposure to others’ designs has negligible effect on their purchase and project creation behaviors. We also conduct the same analysis by interacting the customer’s past purchase indicator14with the treatment group indicator and confirm that the results are consistent (Table C.1 in Online Appendix C). Overall, our results show that social learning is significantly more effective for customers with prior experience on prosumption (Hypothesis 2).15 Table 4.Moderating Effect of Customer Past Project Creation Behavior (Experiment 1) Table 4.Moderating Effect of Customer Past Project Creation Behavior (Experiment 1) Note.Robust standard errors are in parentheses. *, **, and ***Significant at 10%, 5%, and 1%, respectively. Our results are economically significant. FromTable 3, we learned that showing other users’ designs as examples can increase the revenue by 84 cents for each visitor. More importantly, the results inTable 4show that the main effect happens from users with prior experience and can increase the revenue by $5 per experienced users. This is about 10% of the average purchase price on the platform. To ensure that our results are robust, we estimate our model by using alternative model specifications as well as an alternative dependent variable. As shown in Table C.2 in Online Appendix C, we find that our results are consistent using Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) using a log-transformed dependent variable. 6.1.3. Effect of Image Characteristics. To shed light on the role of two mechanisms underlying the observed main effect and to offer implications on the best type of user designs to display, we further explore the effectiveness of different types of project design features in driving customers’ prosumption behaviors. As discussed inHypotheses 3and4, image quality and level of difficulty in others’ designs may affect customers’ purchase and project creation behaviors through different mechanisms; image quality will likely affect users’ initial decision to engage in the design process, whereas image difficulty may govern their likelihood of project completion and eventual purchase. To examine the causal effect of different characteristics in an example project design on customers’ purchase and project creation behaviors, we explore the exogenous variations we created at the feature level using Experiment 1. As discussed inSection 4.2, all customers in our experiment who are randomly allocated to the treatment groups also went through another randomization process: They saw three designs randomly chosen from the 74 projects designed by other users. We characterize such high-dimensional treatment at an individual level as a bundle of image features; that is, each subject encounters a different bundle of features. Specifically, as discussed inSection 5.1, we construct quality and difficulty variables for each user based on the set of gallery images to which they were exposed. In addition, we standardized each value of these features and ran the model using only users in the treatment group. We report the effect of the different levels of project design features inTable 5. Our results provide supportive evidence aligned with the above argument on the underlying mechanisms. First, we find that customers are more likely to initiate the design process as the quality of the image increases. The results imply that customers form an expectation about the final product based on the quality of the sample image and only will engage in the design process if the expected quality of their own design exceeds a certain threshold. Second, we find that showing others’ designs with a high level of difficulty negatively affects customers’ cocreation behaviors. The results suggest that when skillsets used to create the design are far beyond the reach of focal users, the negative effect of self-efficacy dominates. Third, we find that image quality has an insignificant effect on purchase behaviors. The results imply that once customers experience the prosumption process, they will be less likely to change the expectation of the quality, and difficulty level will have a dominant effect on their purchase behavior. Overall, the results suggest that firms should focus on showing others’ designs that are not only high quality, but also with low-difficulty levels so average focal users can achieve and imitate the example designs. In this way, the firms may leverage social learning to improve feature knowledge effect of focal customers and also take care of their self-efficacy in the prosumption process. Table 5.Effect of Project Design Characteristics (Experiment 1) Table 5.Effect of Project Design Characteristics (Experiment 1) Note.QualityandDifficultyvariables are normalized. *, **, and ***Significant at 10%, 5%, and 1%, respectively. 6.2. Experiment 2 We report the treatment effect of Experiment 2 on the upper stage of purchase funnel inTable 6. The results based on customers without prior experience show that image quality significantly affects customers’ project creation decisions (Table 6, column 1). We find that although exposing customers to a high-quality design significantly increases their likelihood of creating a project, showing low-quality designs significantly decreases their creation likelihood, even when compared with not showing any designs (control group). These patterns are consistent regardless of the level of difficulty of the design. When examining the effect on customers with prior project creation experience, we find that images with low difficulty (i.e., T1 and T3) significantly increase the project creation likelihood compared with the control group. Unlike with customers without prior experience, quality did not have an effect on customers’ decisions to create a project during the creation stage (Table 6, column 2). These results align with our theorization that showing others’ designs helps customers reduce uncertainty about how the final product may look, and image quality plays a crucial role in triggering novel customers to initiate the prosumption process. However, once customers gain experience and resolve some uncertainty around the design process, they focus more on self-efficacy and difficulty level becomes more important (Hypothesis 3). These results also explain why social learning is more effective for customers with prior experience (Table 4); customers with prior experience are less likely to dampen their expectations even when exposed to low-quality images. Table 6.Causal Effect of Showing Other Customers’ Project Designs on the Upper Stage of the Purchase Funnel Table 6.Causal Effect of Showing Other Customers’ Project Designs on the Upper Stage of the Purchase Funnel Note.ProjectCreationandOrderare binary variables. *, **, and ***Significant at 10%, 5%, and 1%, respectively. We further present the effect on customers’ purchase decisions on the lower stage of the purchase funnel inTable 7. The results show that, consistent across the purchase funnel, T3 (high-quality-with-low-difficulty features) significantly increases the purchase decision and outcome compared with the control group. We also report the conversion rate of each group inTable 8. We find that, consistent across the purchase funnel, designs with low-difficulty features lead to higher conversion rates. Again, quality did not have an effect on the conversion rate. These results supportHypothesis 4.16 Table 7.Causal Effect of Showing Other Customers’ Project Designs on the Lower Stage of the Purchase Funnel Table 7.Causal Effect of Showing Other Customers’ Project Designs on the Lower Stage of the Purchase Funnel Note.ProjectCreationandOrderare binary variables. *, **, and ***Significant at 10%, 5%, and 1%, respectively. Table 8.Conversion Rate of Each Group (Experiment 2) Table 8.Conversion Rate of Each Group (Experiment 2) Note.Conversion rate, total number of customers who made a purchase/total number of customers who created a project. We summarize the results of the two experiments inTable 9. Overall, we find that the level of the quality and the difficulty of designs can differently affect customers’ prosumption behaviors across different purchase funnels. Although high-quality designs trigger novel customers to initiate the prosumption process, low-difficulty features in a design play an important role in converting customers to make a purchase regardless of the purchase-funnel stage. The results suggest that social comparisons with other users of comparable status can significantly influence customers’ product designs and purchases. Therefore, firms need to pay special attention and walk a fine line when crowdsourcing users’ designs to facilitate social learning. Table 9.Summary of the Results Table 9.Summary of the Results Showing other users’ designs has a positive effect on project creation and purchase (Hypothesis 1) The positive effect is significantly higher for experienced customers (Hypothesis 2) Showing high-quality designs has a positive effect on project creation Showing low-difficulty designs has a positive effect on purchase Showing high-quality (low-quality) designs has a positive (negative) effect on project creation (difficulty level does not play a role) (Hypothesis 3A) Showing low-difficulty (high-difficulty) designs leads to a higher (lower) conversion rate (Hypothesis 4) Showing easy-to-learn, high-quality designs results in the best outcomes Showing low-difficulty designs has a positive effect on project creation (quality level does not play a role) (Hypothesis 3B) Showing low-difficulty (high-difficulty) designs leads to a higher (lower) conversion rate (Hypothesis 4) Showing easy-to-learn, high-quality designs results in the best outcomes
article.7. Conclusion and Future Research: With the advancement of digital technologies and cocreation infrastructure, customers no longer just make “take it or leave it” decisions. They increasingly adopt the role of prosumers and participate in actual product creation. In product categories ranging from running shoes to photo collages to education programs, consumers have much more control over their own products and services. Nevertheless, how to facilitate the prosumption process and help customers better design the products and overcome different challenges remains a question. In this study, we investigate whether social learning—the act of showing focal users sample creations made by other users—can improve customers’ project creation and purchase decisions. Specifically, we conduct a large-scale randomized field experiment in close collaboration with an eCommerce platform and show that under certain conditions, displaying other users’ designs as examples can be highly effective in encouraging the purchase and design behaviors of focal customers. However, firms must target therightcustomers (with experience in prosumption and with a reasonable expectation of product quality) and carefully select thetypeof user design for display (with high-quality, low-difficulty images). Based on the large number of users who dropped off on the platform, our treatment effect estimates suggest that the platform can increase their revenue by more than $405K and $45K per year by sending out emails with other users’ designs to customers in browsing stage and product-design stage, respectively. Theoretically, we explore the rich heterogeneity in the treatment effect across image features, customer characteristics, and purchase funnels and find evidence that both types of social learning mechanisms—perceived quality and self-efficacy—affect the process. By selectively showing others’ product designs to certain customers, the firm may help those customers learn ideas from peers, reduce their uncertainty in evaluating the product, and improve their self-efficacy. Our study is among the first to test and understand whether and how firms can leverage social learning to improve customer prosumption. The nuanced findings from our study can offer immediate implications to digital platforms in the process of determining whether and how to encourage their customers’ participation in cocreation. There are a few promising directions for future research. First, as consumers increasingly assume roles traditionally performed by professionals, we need a broader understanding of how digital platforms can providesocial interventionsand community-based support for consumers in their self-design process (Boudreau and Lakhani 2013). In our study, we explore a simple form of social intervention—showing others’ designs as examples. Future studies can explore other types of social interventions, such as facilitating direct interaction and communication between customers during the cocreation process. In addition, it would be interesting to think about how to encourage customers to publish their designs publicly so others may see them. Triggering such a social image motive or visibility effect could drive customers to spend more effort and create more and better-quality projects. The effect is not present in our current experiment as the designs used are only selectively crowdsourced rather than fully open to participation from all users. Our study design ensures that customers are not aware of and cannot choose to share their project designs with others even if they want to. In alignment with this idea, the quantity of others’ designs would also affect the prosumption process. Overall, more research is needed to understand the influences that social factors and group dynamics have on both the design process itself and subsequent valuations of the customized product. The other interesting extension of the current study is to understand how professional guidance and support might be incorporated into the cocreation process to facilitate prosumption. The success at Airbnb in introducing professional photographers to help hosts create their online property profiles is an excellent example. The involvement of such professional support is especially crucial if focal customers lack certain skills in the cocreation process and find it fundamentally difficult or costly to learn within a short time; however, previous literature on social comparison has found evidence that showing professionally edited pieces might have a negative effect on individuals’ own creativity (Burroughs et al. 2008). AsMoreau and Herd (2010)demonstrate, consumers will compare their design skills whenever possible. Helping customers avoid upward comparison with professional designers when evaluating their own designs is important. If needed, platforms may even offer options to have professionals directly help customers in their cocreation efforts. Finally, with the advancement of artificial intelligence and computing power, digital platforms can increasingly rely on machines and algorithms to evaluate customers’ designs and offer real-time suggestions. Future research can adopt advanced machine-learning techniques aimed at achieving three goals. First, researchers can use off-the-shelf algorithms to evaluate aesthetics of the project output and discover potential use of technical features. Many tools have started offering templates to lower user friction. Second, researchers may use machine learning to discover the optimal amount of information to reveal to the consumers. Third, researchers may be able to predict individuals’ personal needs based on the individual’s design styles and their previous orders. We envision that much more research can be done in this dimension. In summary, understanding the factors that influence customers’ cocreation behaviors during the prosumption process is becoming more and more critical for online platforms and retailers. Ideally, firms want to identify consumers with stronger potential and need, design marketing strategies to communicate with them, and develop new interfaces and interventions to optimize their prosumption experiences. In this paper, we develop a deeper understanding of the underlying mechanisms and provide more nuanced managerial implications to online platforms and retailers to better facilitate cocreation in the prosumption process. Our study serves as a first step in this direction, and we hope the current work will generate additional interest and research for a better understanding of how online platforms may improve the prosumption process.
